coredata.dc:title: Photodynamic antibacterial activity of oxidase-like nanozyme based on long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xiaojie
Luo, Shiqing
Zhang, Lifang
Miao, Yanming
Yan, Guiqin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon dots (P-CDs) with the properties of ultraviolet/visible (UV/Vis) light photoresponsive oxidase-like nanozyme were synthesized from diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid and through a one-step hydrothermal method. P-CDs were used as a light-driven oxidative-like enzyme for antimicrobial studies. The results showed that under UV/Vis light irradiation, P-CDs could efficiently convert O2 into 1O2, and the strong oxidizing property of 1O2 greatly enhanced the growth inhibition of P-CDs on Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli). Meanwhile, P-CDs exhibited good photodynamic antifungal properties against Botrytis cinerea (B. cinerea). Then the P-CDs were made into P-CDs/PVA films, which effectively prolonged the preservation period of fruits under photodynamic antibacterial action. The good biocompatibility and efficient photosensitive oxygen activation can make P-CDs a more practically useful oxidase-like nanozyme.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent
Carbon dots
Nanozyme
Photodynamic antibacterial
Fruit preservation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoresponsive oxidase activity and steady-state kinetics of P-CDs
Mechanism on light-responsive oxidase-mimicking activity of P-CDs
Measurement of singlet oxygen (1O2) quantum yield
Application of P-CDs/PVA films in photodynamic antibacterial fruit preservation
Mechanism of the photoresponsive oxidase-mimicking activity of P-CDs
Application of P-CDs/PVA films into fruit preservation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduate Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Normal University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was completed with the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) ( 32171393 , 31700862 ), the Shanxi Postgraduate Education Innovation Project (2020BY083), and the Graduate Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Normal University (2019XBY014).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of P-CDs. (B) size distribution of P-CDs. (C) XRD patterns of P-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectrum of the P-CDs. (E) XPS survey spectra ofP-CDs. (F) UV–vis absorption spectra (black line), optimal fluorescence excitation (orange line) and emission (blue line) spectra of the P-CDs in water solutions. (Inset: photograph of P-CDs aqueous solution under UV light). (G) optimal RTP excitation (orange line) and emission (green line) spectra of P-CDs solids. (H) 3D RTP contour map of powder P-CDs. (I) RTP decay spectra of powder P-CDs.

(A) UV-vis absorption spectra of different reaction systems. Effects of (B) pH (ultrapure water (UPW, pH=6.2); curves a: TMB+UV; curves b: TMB+P-CDs+UV; b-a: Difference in absorbance with and without P-CDs), (C) reaction temperature, (D) P-CD concentration, and (E) TMB concentration on the oxidase activity of P-CDs after 5min of UV light irradiation. Inset shows the photos of corresponding samples. (F) UV light and (G) Vis light photocontrollable oxidase activity of P-CDs. The Michaelis-Menten curves of the P-CDs catalytic activity with TMB as the substrate under (H) UV light or (I) Vis light. Inset: corresponding Lineweaver-Burk images.

Effect of (A) UV and (B) Vis light irradiation with different trapping agents on the oxidation of TMB by P-CDs. EPR spectra of 1O2 generation from P-CDs under (C) 5min of UV light irradiation or (D) 10min of Vis light irradiation. (E) Changes in the adsorption spectra under UV light irradiation at different time intervals in the P-CDs and DPBF (30μM) water mixtures. (F) Changes in the adsorption spectra under UV light irradiation at different time intervals in the PB and DPBF (30μM) water mixtures. (G) Mechanistic diagram of P-CDs catalyzed TMB oxidation.

Colonies of (A) E. coli and (B) S. aureus, and survival rates of (C) E. coli and (D) S. aureus with different P-CDs concentrations with or without illumination. SEM images of E. coli in (E) control group, (F) P-CDs, (G) P-CDs/Vis, and (H) P-CDs/UV treatments. SEM images of S. aureus in (I) control group, (J) P-CDs, (K) P-CDs/Vis, and (L) P-CDs/UV treatments. The irradiation conditions were: UV light for 5 min, and Vis light for 30 min.
Application of P-CDs/PVA films into photodynamic antibacterial fruit preservation. (A) appearance of fruits during storage; weight loss rates of (B) strawberry and (C) banana.
Schematic diagram of the preparation, the photodynamic antibacterial process, and the application of P-CDs in photodynamic antibacterial fruit freshness preservation.

head.ce:title: Photodynamic antibacterial activity of oxidase-like nanozyme based on long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Materials Science of Shanxi Normal University & Collaborative Innovation Center for Shanxi Advanced Permanent Magnetic Materials and Technology, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Materials Science of Shanxi Normal University & Collaborative Innovation Center for Shanxi Advanced Permanent Magnetic Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Materials Science of Shanxi Normal University & Collaborative Innovation Center for Shanxi Advanced Permanent Magnetic Materials and Technology, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, China (L. Zhang). School of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, China (Y. Miao).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A long-lived room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon quantum dots (P-CDs) with ultraviolet/visible (UV/Vis) light photoresponsive oxidase-like nanozyme were synthesized to achieve higher photosensitive oxygen activation. Then the P-CDs were made into P-CDs/PVA films, which effectively prolonged the preservation period of fruits under photodynamic antibacterial action.

In this study, a novel long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon dots (P-CDs) with the properties of ultraviolet/visible (UV/Vis) light photoresponsive oxidase-like nanozyme were synthesized from diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid and through a one-step hydrothermal method. P-CDs were used as a light-driven oxidative-like enzyme for antimicrobial studies. The results showed that under UV/Vis light irradiation, P-CDs could efficiently convert O2 into 1O2, and the strong oxidizing property of 1O2 greatly enhanced the growth inhibition of P-CDs on Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli). Meanwhile, P-CDs exhibited good photodynamic antifungal properties against Botrytis cinerea (B. cinerea). Then the P-CDs were made into P-CDs/PVA films, which effectively prolonged the preservation period of fruits under photodynamic antibacterial action. The good biocompatibility and efficient photosensitive oxygen activation can make P-CDs a more practically useful oxidase-like nanozyme.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: 1O2 has the characteristics of easy formation and high oxidation activity, and is the core molecule in the photodynamic effect. As the most abundant and green oxidant, oxygen (O2) is the main molecule to produce 1O2. However, since O2 has a triplet ground state (3O2, spin forbidden), O2 is kinetically stable, and it is usually necessary to overcome the spin forbidden by photosensitizers to convert triplet oxygen (3O2) to singlet state (1O2). However, the commonly used photosensitizers such as organic dyes (Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017), and nanomaterials, such as nano-carbon nitride (Zhou, Zhang, Shen, Liu, & Zhang, 2018), nano-graphene (Ge et al., 2014), metal-organic frameworks (Zheng, Wang, Pei, He, Liu, & Xie, 2017), nano-black phosphorus (Wang et al., 2015), etc., have low photosensitivity efficiency, resulting in low quantum yield of 1O2, and cumbersome preparation process or poor water solubility, which has become the bottleneck of their application in photodynamic antibacterial. Hence, it is necessary to develop novel photosensitizers with higher quantum yields of 1O2. Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) nano photosensitizer provides a new approach to solve this problem.

Since RTP comes from the triple excited state (T1) transition (Miao, Liu, Ma, Yang, Li, & Lv, 2021; Zhang et al., 2018), photosensitizers with RTP properties have the characteristic of high quantum yield of T1 excitons. Since O2 is a triplet ground state, RTP photosensitizers can generate higher-oxidizing 1O2 through the energy transfer between T1-state exciton and O2 molecules (Knoblauch et al., 2021; Ogilby, 2010; Zhang et al., 2018). In addition, since intermolecular collisions are the main route of energy transfer, the longer lifetime of T1 excitons is conducive to the energy transfer of T1 excitons to O2 through longer molecular collisions, which is conducive to the improvement of 1O2 quantum yield. Hence, RTP photosensitizers with longer lifetimes have higher 1O2 quantum yields (R. Gao, Mei, Yan, Liang, & Wei, 2018; Xu et al., 2020). However, the RTP photosensitizers used in 1O2 generation are mainly Pt(II)porphyrin (Finikova, Cheprakov, & Vinogradov, 2005), Ru(II) compounds (Neugebauer et al., 2008) and Ir(III) compounds (Zhang et al., 2010), which all have some defects, such as low water solubility, ease of aggregation, unstable chemical properties, ease of optical decomposition, dark toxicity, high costs, and presence of metals. In comparison, RTP P-CDs can overcome the defects of traditional RTP photosensitizers.

Compared with other photosensitizers, carbon dots (CDs) have lower cost, lower cytotoxicity, higher water solubility and higher biocompatibility (Ge et al., 2021; Wu, Abbas, Yang, Li, Tedesco, & Bi, 2022) and thus become a potential type of photo-driven oxidase-like nanozymes. An efficient photo-driven oxidase-like nanozyme shall have high yield and long lifetime of T1, but the yield and lifetime of T1 are directly related to the yield and lifetime of RTP. Thus, the photo-driven oxidase-like nanozyme that stimulates efficient 1O2 generation and has high RTP yield and long RTP lifetime may be a promising route. Long-lived RTP CDs have high RTP yield and longer RTP lifetime, and thus are the first-choice materials to improve 1O2 quantum yields.

Herein, long-lived RTP carbon dots (P-CDs) with good water solubility were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) as a single material and used as photo-driven oxidase-like substances into antibacterial experiments. The P-CDs under UV (365 nm) or Vis light irradiation can significantly inhibit Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). Moreover, the application potential of the oxidase-like P-CDs in photodynamic antibacterial was tested. Furthermore, P-CDs/PVA films were prepared, and used in strawberry and banana storing experiments (Scheme 1 ) to evaluate the photo-assisted fruit preservation ability of the P-CDs/PVA films and their possible use in food packing.
The details of materials and instruments were shown in Supporting Information.

P-CDs were synthesized according to Han et al. (2015) with some modifications. First, 0.79 g of DTPA was mixed with 30 mL of ultrapure water, then transferred to a reaction kettle and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the clarified solution was collected through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane filter, dialyzed with dialysis bags (MWCO: 500–1000), and freeze-dried to obtain P-CDs.

Furthermore, P-CDs/PVA composite films were prepared to better study the RTP of the P-CDs. Specifically, 0.25 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was dissolved into 2.5 mL of ultrapure water, and then mixed with 100 μL of P-CDs solution (2.5 mg mL−1). After evenly mixing, a P-CDs/PVA solution was formed. Finally, the P-CDs/PVA solution was coated onto a glass substrate plate, which was dried for hours to form P-CDs/PVA composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoresponsive oxidase activity and steady-state kinetics of P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The light-activated oxidase-like activity of P-CDs was investigated using TMB as a substrate molecule. In general,200 μL of P-CDs solution (10 mg mL−1) and 200 μL of TMB solution (10 mM) were mixed into the colorimetric tube. The final volume of the mixed solution was adjusted to 2.0 mL with the ultrapure water.The above solutions were irradiated with UV light (365 nm, 7.22 mW·cm−2) or Vis light (7.15 mW·cm−2) at room temperature, and the absorbance at 652 nm was collected. Each group of experiments was repeated three times.

With TMB as the substrate and under the optimum conditions, the steady-state kinetics of P-CDs were studied by changing the concentration of TMB. The kinetic parameters were computed based on the Michaelis-Menten equation:
V = V
max
×
[ S ]
/
(
K m
+
[ S ]
)
where Km is the Michaelis constant, V is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximum reaction velocity, and [S] is the substrate concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism on light-responsive oxidase-mimicking activity of P-CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the mechanism of the light-induced oxidase-mimicking activity of P-CDs, we conducted reactive oxygen species (ROS) capture experiments. Into the P-CDs/TMB system under the optimum conditions, different trapping agents were added, including 5 mM mannitol (
OH scavenger), 2.5 mg mL−1 tryptophane (1O2 scavenger), 200 U mL−1 hydrogen peroxidase (H2O2 scavenger), 300 U mL−1 superoxide dismutase (O2
– scavenger), or 10 % vt ethanol (h+ scavenger). Then, the mixed solutions were irradiated under UV or Vis light for different times, and the absorbance at 652 nm was collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of singlet oxygen (1O2) quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield of 1O2 was measured with DPBF and using phloxine B (PB) as the standard photosensitizer (ΦΔPB=0.65 in water). Into a DPBF (30 μM) solution, PB or P-CDs at an appropriate concentration was added to ensure there was adequate absorbance at 365 nm in the mixtures. Afterwards, the above solution was irradiated with ultraviolet light, and the UV–Vis spectra at different time periods were recorded. The relative 1O2 quantum yield of P-CDs was computed using Eqs. (1) and (2) (Xu et al., 2021; Zhu, Xu, Zhang, Ding, Yu, & Xu, 2018):
(1)
Φ
Δ P - C D s
=
Φ
Δ P B
×
K
P - C D s
×
I
P B
/
(
K
P B
×
I
P - C D s
)
(2)
I
P B
/
I
P - C D s
=
( 1 -
10
- A
365
P B
)
/
( 1 -
10
- A
365
P - C D s
)
where ΦΔ is the 1O2 quantum yield; K P-CDs and K PB are the decomposition rate constants of DPBF by the P-CDs and PB, respectively; I PB and I P-CDs are the optical absorption rates of PB and P-CDs at an irradiation wavelength of 365 nm, respectively.
The details of the Photodynamic antibacterial research of P-CDs were described in Supporting Materials.

The morphological changes of the bacteria were observed under SEM. After the antibacterial experiments, the bacteria were collected via centrifugation (3000–4000 rpm) and fixed in glutaraldehyde (2.5 %) at 4 °C overnight. The bacteria were washed with PBS (pH 7.4) and dehydrated stepwise with gradient ethanol solutions. The resulting samples were fully dried and the bacterial morphology was observed under SEM.
This section presents in Supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of P-CDs/PVA films in photodynamic antibacterial fruit preservation

ce:section.ce:para: Powder PVA (5 g) was dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water, and then stirred in a 95 °C water bath for 30 min, forming a 5 % PVA solution. The above solution was mixed with glycerol (the weight of glycerol is 20 % of the dry weight of PVA) and finally added with the P-CDs (50 μg mL−1) solution, forming a P-CDs/PVA solution after uniform stirring.

Strawberries and bananas were chosen here. First, the P-CDs/PVA solution was brushed on the bananas, and the strawberries were soaked into the P-CDs/PVA solution for about 1 min. Then the fruits were irradiated under UV light for 5 min or Vis light for 30 min, and stored at room temperature. Each preservation experiment was done in triplicate. The typical images during the storage were recorded. With appearance and weight loss rate as the indices, the preservation effect of the P-CDs/PVA films on the fruits under photodynamic antibacterial conditions was tested.

The TEM diagram of Fig. 1 A shows that P-CDs resemble a spherical structure with an average particle size of ∼ 4.1 nm (Fig. 1B) and without evident lattice, suggesting that the P-CDs may be a polymer. The structures were further observed under XRD, which showed a distinct wide peak at 22° (Fig. 1C), further demonstrating the P-CDs are an amorphous polymer. The zeta potential of the P-CDs was 13.7 mV (in ultrapure water).

The surface groups of P-CDs were analyzed via FTIR and XPS. The wide characteristic peak at about 3442 cm−1 (Fig. 1D) is ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH groups. The small characteristic peaks at 3024 cm−1 and 1729 cm−1 are derived from the bending vibration of CH and the stretching vibration of COOH, respectively. The stretching vibration peak of CC/CN is at 1632 cm−1, and the characteristic peaks at 1399 cm−1 and 1238 cm−1 are derived from the stretching vibration of COC and C-OH, respectively. As shown in the XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E), the three characteristic peaks of P-CDs at 285.0 eV, 402.1 eV, and 532.1 eV correspond to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The C 1s bands (Fig. S1A) can be decomposed into three characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV (CC/CC), 286.2 eV (CN/CO), and 288.6 eV (COOH). The N 1s bands (Fig. S1B) can be resolved into two characteristic peaks at 399.2 eV (pyrrole nitrogen) and 401.7 eV (NH). O 1s (Fig. S1C) was divided into two characteristic peaks at 531.5 eV (CO) and 533.1 eV (C-OH). The results of FTIR and XPS indicated that there were abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of P-CDs, which can promote the protonation of P-CDs.

The optimal fluorescence excitation peak and emission peak of the P-CDs were 335 nm and 410 nm, respectively (Fig. 1F and Fig. S1D). The P-CDs water solution showed blue fluorescence under UV illumination at 365 nm (inset in Fig. 1F). The fluorescent lifetime of the P-CDs was 5.81 ns (Fig. S1E), and the absolute fluorescent quantum yield was 12 %.

The optimal RTP excitation peak and emission peak of P-CDs were 295 nm and 465 nm, respectively (Fig. 1G, 1H). Under the same excitation conditions (295 nm), the RTP emission wavelength of the P-CDs in the substrate PVA (Fig. S1F) was consistent with the RTP emission of P-CDs powder (Fig. 1G). In order to explore the source of RTP of P-CDs, we compared the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1F) and RTP excitation spectrum of P-CDs (Fig. 1G). P-CDs showed a broad absorption band at 280–450 nm (Fig. 1F), which could be attributed to the n-π* transition of CO/CN bonds (Deng, Zhao, Chen, Wang, Song, & Shen, 2013; Jiang, Hu, Wang, Li, & Lin, 2020), because the existence of CO/CN bonds may promote effective intersystem crossing (ISC), which is favorable for the increase of T1 and the generation of RTP (Jiang, Wang, Gao, Cai, & Lin, 2018b; Nie, Lai, Zheng, & Weng, 2021; Wang et al., 2021). The RTP excitation spectrum of P-CDs overlaps with the adsorption band (275–450 nm) of P-CDs, suggesting that the RTP of P-CDs may originate from the CO/CN group on P-CDs. The RTP decay spectra (Fig. 1I) of P-CDs were fitted into a three-exponent function with three lifetimes of 16.60 ms (20.01 %), 109.53 ms (42.28 %) and 554.54 ms (37.71 %), suggesting there were multiple delay channels. The average phosphorescence lifetime of P-CDs is 258.75 ms.

On the basis of the above results, the phosphorescent mechanism of P-CDs may be as follows. CO/CN groups are generated during the formation of P-CDs and act as the phosphorescent luminescence centers (Gao, Zhang, Shuang, & Dong, 2020; Jiang, Wang, Cai, & Lin, 2018a). Meanwhile, the presence of N element in DTPA promotes the n-π* transition, which is more conducive to the spin-forbidden transition from the singlet excited state (S1) to the T1 (Cook & Stang, 2015; Y. Gao et al., 2018), which promotes the phosphorescent emission of P-CDs.

TMB was chosen as a colorimetric substrate to evaluate the light-responsive oxidase-like catalytic ability of P-CDs. The P-CDs, under UV light or Vis light, catalyzed the oxidation of TMB and produced a blue color, and the oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) showed an obvious characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm (Fig. 2 A). With the absence of light or P-CDs, the solution was colorless and had no obvious absorption peak at 652 nm. These results indicate that P-CDs have UV/Vis light-triggered oxidase-like activities.

Like natural enzymes, the catalytic performances of nanozymes also depend on pH, reaction temperature, P-CD concentration and TMB concentration. As shown in Fig. 2B and S2A, under UV/Vis irradiation, the absorbance of the pure TMB solution (curve a) showed a decreasing trend (the solution gradually changed from blue to colorless) with increasing pH (3.8–5.8). Under UV light (Fig. 2B), after adding P-CDs to the TMB solution (curve b), the absorbance remained relatively stable in the pH (3.8–5.0) range and decreased in the pH (5.0–5.8) range. Under Vis light (Fig. S2A), after adding P-CDs to the TMB solution (curve b), the absorbance first increased and then decreased with the increase of pH (3.8–5.8). When the acetic acid/sodium acetate buffer solution was replaced with ultrapure water (UPW, pH = 6.2), under UV/Vis irradiation, the pure TMB solution did not change in color and its absorbance was low. After adding P-CDs to the TMB solution, the solution turned dark blue and its absorbance was enhanced. The absorbance value of the solution before and after adding P-CDs was compared (curves b-a in Fig. 2B, curves b-a in Fig. S2A), which showed when ultrapure water (pH = 6.2) was used as the buffer solvent, the difference in absorbance was larger, and the solution color contrast was significant. Hence, ultrapure water (pH = 6.2) was chosen as the reaction environment. The oxidative capacity of P-CDs was not much variable with temperature (Fig. 2C and Fig. S2B). When the concentration of P-CDs increased to 1.0 mg mL−1, the ability of P-CDs to catalyze the oxidation of TMB was the strongest (Fig. 2D and Fig. S2C). As the concentration of TMB rose, the catalytic ability of P-CDs was gradually increased and stabilized at TMB concentration of 1.0 mM (Fig. 2E and Fig. S2D). Furthermore, even after 2 months of storage, P-CDs maintained more than 90 % of the photoresponsive oxidase activity (Fig. S3).

When the UV light (Fig. 2F) or Vis light (Fig. 2G) irradiation was successivelyswitched on or off, the absorbance of P-CDs at 652 nm showed a staircase-like response, demonstrating P-CDs have photoresponsive oxidase-mimicking activity.

To further evaluate the light-responsive oxidase-mimicking activity of P-CDs, the typical Michaelis-Menten curve under the optimum conditions was acquired by changing the TMB concentration (Fig. 2H, 2I). The steady-state kinetic parameters of P-CDs under UV light irradiation were computed (inset in Fig. 2H). The Km and Vmax were 0.17 mM and 5.00 × 10-8 M·s−1, respectively. The Km and Vmax under Vis light irradiation were 0.40 mM and 0.60 × 10-8 M·s−1, respectively (inset in Fig. 2I). Compared with other nanozymes (Table S1), the Km of P-CDs is relatively small and Vmax is higher, suggesting that P-CDs possess agood oxidase-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the photoresponsive oxidase-mimicking activity of P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the catalytic mechanism of P-CDs, we performed capture experiments to determine the possible reactive species generated during the oxidation process, including singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radical (
OH), superoxide anion (O2
-), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and photogenerated hole (h+). The absorption intensity decreased significantly with the prolonging of irradiation time, only after tryptophane was added (Fig. 3 A, 3B), indicating that the photosensitive oxidation process was mediated by 1O2. In addition, EPR spectra further confirmed the production of 1O2 during the catalytic oxidation (Fig. 3C, 3D). DPBF was used to assess the 1O2 generation ability of P-CDs. The absorbance of the mixed solution of P-CDs and DPBF at 415 nm gradually decreased with increasing illumination time (Fig. 3E and Fig. S4 A), indicating the 1O2 production was enhanced slowly with the prolonged irradiation time. To assess the 1O2 quantum yield of P-CDs, PB was chosen as a standard photosensitizer (Fig. 3F) (ΦΔPB=0.65 in water) (Zhang et al., 2018). According to Fig. S4B, Fig. S4C, Fig. S4D and Fig. S4E, the 1O2 quantum yield of P-CDs was computed with Eqs. (1) and (2) to be 0.64. In addition, the effect of dissolved oxygen in the system on the catalytic oxidation of TMB by P-CDs was further studied. During oxygen deficiency, the peak intensity at 652 nm sharply decreased (Fig. S5A, S5B), suggesting dissolved oxygen participated as a reactant in photocatalytic oxidation.

On the basis of the above results, the possible mechanism of photoresponsive oxidase-mimicking activity of P-CDs is shown below (Fig. 3G). After P-CDs absorbed photons, the P-CDs were excited to the single excited state (S1). S1 emitted fluorescence to return to the ground state (S0) and transferred via intersystem crossing (ISC) to the T1 (Celli et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2016). Then T1 emitted phosphorescence and decayed to its ground state. Moreover, T1 can collide with the triplet-state dissolved O2 to transfer energy to O2 molecules, thereby driving 1O2 generation (DeRosa & Crutchley, 2002; W. Miao et al., 2021; Plaetzer, Krammer, Berlanda, Berr, & Kiesslich, 2009). Finally, 1O2 oxidized TMB, which became blue ox-TMB.

As is well-known, ROS (e.g.,
OH, O2
–, H2O2, 1O2) is pivotal in the metabolism of organisms, but excessive ROS will severely damage cell membrane lipids, DNA and organelles, leading to cell death. P-CDs, with excellent photoresponsive oxidase-like activity, can efficiently produce sufficient 1O2 and thus are a new strategy for effective antibacterial. Herein, the photodynamic antibacterial effects of P-CDs on E. coli and S. aureus were studied.

With the absence of P-CDs, the bacterial colonies in the irradiated and dark groups did not change significantly (Fig. 4A, 4B), indicating the effect of illumination on bacterial extinguishing is negligible. P-CDs alone had a weak antibacterial effect on E. coli and S. aureus. However, after adding irradiation, the antibacterial activity of P-CDs was significantly enhanced, and the bacterial colonies of the two species both decreased abruptly. Moreover, the antibacterial effect of P-CDs on S. aureus was stronger under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4C, when the P-CDs concentration was 0, 5, 10, 50 and 100 μg mL−1, the survival rates of E. coli in the dark were 100.00 %, 99.25 %, 89.87 %, 84.32 % and 76.76 %, respectively. After 30 min of Vis light irradiation, the survival rates of E. coli dropped to 99.10 %, 81.50 %, 55.41 %, 22.39 % and 4.20 %, respectively. After 5 min of UV light irradiation, the survival rates of E. coli declined to 99.84 %, 70.40 %, 22.48 %, 12.54 % and 0.75 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4D, when the P-CDs concentration was 0, 5, 10, 20 and 30 μg mL−1, the survival rates of S. aureus in the dark were 100.00 %, 91.84 %, 87.58 %, 75.40 % and 65.57 %, respectively. After 30 min of Vis light irradiation, the survival rates of S. aureus were 100.30%, 53.64 % %, 23.29 %, 6.73 % and 0.91 %, respectively. After 5 min of UV light irradiation, the survival rates of S. aureus were 98.45 %, 38.23 %, 21.07 %, 2.53 % and 0.09 %, respectively.

Furthermore, the photodynamic antifungal properties of P-CDs were further evaluated using Botrytis cinerea (B. cinerea) as the research subject. As shown in Figure S6, in the absence of P-CDs, UV/Vis irradiation had little or no killing effect on the fungus. P-CDs alone showed weak antifungal activity against B. cinerea. The inhibition rate of 2 mg mL-1P-CDs against B. cinerea was 24.26 % on the second day of incubation and 24.72 % on the fourth day. After adding irradiation, the antimicrobial performance of P-CDs was significantly enhanced. On the second day of cultivation, under Vis light and UV light, the inhibition rates of 2 mg mL-1P-CDs against B. cinerea were 62.45 % and 64.39 %, respectively. On the fourth day of cultivation, under Vis light and UV light, the inhibition rates of 2 mg mL-1P-CDs against B. cinerea were 63.44 % and 63.83 %, respectively. The above results indicate that P-CDs possess broad-spectrum antibacterial and antifungal properties, with illumination conditions notably enhancing their bactericidal performance.

The morphological changes of the bacteria were characterized by SEM (Fig. 4). The original E. coli (Fig. 4E) and S. aureus (Fig. 4I) were fully rod-shaped and spherical, respectively, and had smooth surfaces and complete bacterial structures. After treatment with P-CDs (Fig. 4F, 4 J), the bacteria deformed, and suffered folds and damages on the surface. The antibacterial activity of P-CDs alone may be caused by the positively-charged N-part (P-CDs amine/amide protonation) on the surface of P-CDs (Travlou, Giannakoudakis, Algarra, Labella, Rodríguez-Castellón, & Bandosz, 2018), which enhances the electrostatic interaction with the negatively-charged cell walls (Wu, Li, van der Mei, Busscher, & Ren, 2021), leading to the peri-bacterial accumulation of P-CDs and inhibiting matter exchange between the bacteria and the environment. After irradiation with P-CDs (Fig. 4G, 4H, 4 K, 4L), the bacterial morphology was fully destroyed, and surface sags, cell wall and/or membrane rupturing, and leakage and accumulation of intracellular matter occurred. Additionally, because of the structural difference in cell walls between Gram-negative bacteria and Gram-positive bacteria, the S. aureus with a porous peptidoglycan and lipoteichoic acid layer was more susceptible to injuries than the E. coli with a multilayer outer surface (Travlou et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2016). The lower isoelectric point on the surface of Gram-positive bacteria compared with Gram-negative bacteria (2–3 vs. 4–5) makes P-CDs easily bind with Gram-positive bacteria, which improves the antimicrobial effects.

We further tested whether P-CDs can inactivate bacteria via the photothermic mechanism. The photothermic effects were directly evaluated with the thermography of P-CDs and ultrapure water under UV/Vis irradiation. P-CDs and ultrapure water showed the same temperature increase trend, and increased to 36 °C and 38 °C after 5 min of UV light irradiation and 30 min of Vis light irradiation, respectively (Fig. S7A, S7B), which proves the P-CDs have no photothermic ability (Nie et al., 2020). Thus, the probability of bacterial inactivation via a pure photothermic mechanism is eliminated.

In all, the P-CDs with oxidase-like activity can effectively generate 1O2 under illumination, and then 1O2 oxidizes the cell walls and/or cell membranes of bacteria, inducing peroxidation and changing the fluidity and permeability of cell membranes, leading to changes in cellular structures and functions. Moreover, the surface positively-charged P-CDs can better and more closely bind with the negatively-charged bacteria via electrostatic interaction, which may also be one of the important reasons for the improved antibacterial ability of P-CDs. Thus, the excellent photodynamic antibacterial property of P-CDs is ascribed to the synergistic effect of electrostatic adsorption and 1O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of P-CDs/PVA films into fruit preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Given the outstanding photodynamic antibacterial ability of P-CDs, we further studied the potential application of the P-CDs/PVA films into photodynamic antibacterial fruit preservation (strawberry, banana). As shown in Fig. 5 A and Figure S8, after placement for 3 days, the fruits (no-film groups, UV groups, PVA film groups) started to shrink, the color turned brown and gradually rotted. After 5 days, the fruits (P-CDs/PVA film groups) started to shrink and gradually rotted. In comparison, the fruits treated by P-CDs/PVA + illumination did not contract or change color until the ninth day, indicating the P-CDs/PVA + illumination group showed a better preservation effect than the other groups. It is suggested with the help of UV or Vis light, the P-CDs/PVA films have better antibacterial activity and can delay fruit deterioration.

To illustrate the preservation effect, we measured the weight loss rates of the fruits (Fig. 5B, 5C). On the ninth day, the strawberries and bananas in the P-CDs/PVA + UV groups reserved about 70 % and 91 % of the original weights, respectively, but the weights of the film-free groups and the PVA film groups significantly dropped. These results further confirm that P-CDs have excellent photodynamic antibacterial effects, and P-CDs/PVA films can decelerate moisture evaporation and respiration. Hence, the processing with P-CDs/PVA film + illumination can effectively prolong the preservation period of fruits. Unlike traditional active films, the antibacterial activity of P-CDs/PVA films can be self-stimulated by optical reactions, thus achieving continual antibacterial effect.

CCK-8 assay was used to evaluate the viability of NIH3T3 cells with different concentrations of P-CDs in darkness or under light irradiation.As shown in Figure S9, cytotoxicity was negligible after P-CDs treatment in the dark, and cell viability was greater than 88 % even with a P-CDs concentration of 100 μg mL−1. When the concentration of P-CDs was 100 μg mL−1, the cell viability after Vis light and UV light irradiation was 88 % and 85 %, respectively. The results indicated that P-CDs had good biocompatibility and negligible phototoxicity.

In summary, long-lived positively-charged P-CDs were prepared. The P-CDs showed excellent photoresponsive oxidase-like catalytic abilityand UV/Vis-light photodynamic antibacterial properties. Fruit preservation experiments show the P-CDs/PVA films can be potentially used as photodynamic antibacterial packing materials to prolong fruit preservation time. These films exhibit wide application prospects in food preservation. The P-CDs were also outstanding with low cost, ease of preparation, high biocompatibility, and unlikelihood of forming bacterial resistance and thus are among the candidate materials to replace antibiotics. Moreover, the P-CDs can be potentially used in wound infection treatment, various antibacterial surfaces, and biological interfaces.

Xiaojie Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Resources. Shiqing Luo: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Validation. Lifang Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yanming Miao: Methodology, Conceptualization, Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Guiqin Yan: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was completed with the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (32171393, 31700862), the Shanxi Postgraduate Education Innovation Project (2020BY083), and the Graduate Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Normal University (2019XBY014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137541.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
